surface.name: Automate: run your first Cypress test (Kitchen Sink) on a public URL
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Committed to reconstructed surface `Unresolved BrowserStack product, framework, language, and target, with Automate Cypress Kitchen Sink on a public URL as the representative candidate`. Prior: BrowserStack offers manual web testing, browser automation, mobile manual and automation, accessibility, low-code automation, visual testing, load testing, test management, reporting, and AI products. Automate then requires a framework and language choice. The official Run your first Cypress test guide is preserved in full because it provides a concrete sample, public test URL, CLI execution, local result files, and Automate dashboard verification. BrowserStack does not establish Cypress as the company-wide default.

alternatives_considered.name: Automate with Selenium
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: BrowserStack labels Selenium widely used, but the path further branches by language, framework, build tool, sample versus existing suite, and local versus public app.

alternatives_considered.name: Automate with Playwright
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: A peer browser-automation framework with different SDK and runner setup.

alternatives_considered.name: App Automate
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Mobile automation requires Appium, Espresso, XCUITest, Flutter, Detox, Maestro, or Smart TV plus an app artifact or URL.

alternatives_considered.name: Live and App Live
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Manual testing starts a browser or real-device session rather than executing an automated build.

alternatives_considered.name: Low Code Automation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: This downloads a desktop recorder, records a test, saves a local replay, creates a suite, and runs in the cloud.

alternatives_considered.name: Accessibility Testing
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Accessibility has separate workflow-analyzer, automation, scanner, screen-reader, and framework paths.

entry_point.developer_intent: Choose one BrowserStack product and first result; the representative route runs the Cypress Kitchen Sink sample against its public URL and inspects the cloud result.
assumed_prior_state: The developer does not have a BrowserStack account, plan, Username, or Access key.
For the representative route, Node.js, Git, the BrowserStack Cypress CLI, sample repository, and configuration are not assumed.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: In the selected Run your first Cypress test route, first success is demonstrated when Automate shows the completed run grouped by browser with a detailed view containing video and text logs.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: Obtain BrowserStack credentials, install the Cypress CLI, clone and configure the Kitchen Sink sample for the public app, execute it on BrowserStack, and inspect its cloud build and logs.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The CLI reports completed test statuses, the results folder contains HTML and JSON build summaries, and Automate groups the run by browser combination with a detailed view containing video and text logs.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: Video and text logs for the executed test are accessible.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Committed under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the reconstructed official path.

prerequisites.requirement: A BrowserStack Free Account or purchased plan with access to Automate. The current signup page offers Google sign-in or a full-name, business-email, and password route and requires acceptance of the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy. The signup page advertises 100 automated browser-testing minutes, while the current free-trial FAQ states 60 Automate minutes.
BrowserStack Username and Access key from account settings.
Basic Cypress fundamentals for the representative route.
Node.js version 12 or newer with npm, plus Git and a text editor or IDE.
Network access to npm, GitHub, https://example.cypress.io, BrowserStack APIs, Automate workers, and dashboard.
Permission to install a global npm CLI, clone a repository, install project packages, and store BrowserStack credentials in the local sample configuration.

primary_path.action: Open BrowserStack documentation and choose the product and testing mode.
details: The site exposes manual web and mobile testing, browser and app automation, accessibility, low-code automation, visual, load, management, reporting, and AI products.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer names web automation as the intended mode.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The roster does not identify a product.

primary_path.action: Choose Automate, then Cypress, then Run your first test.
details: Automate also offers Selenium, Playwright, JS Testing API, and Puppeteer.
The Cypress guide uses the Kitchen Sink example.
primary_path.success_signal: The guide's prerequisites, run, and result sections are selected.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Another framework changes dependencies, SDK, config, and command.

primary_path.action: Open BrowserStack's Create FREE Account page and choose Google or the email-and-password route.
details: The Cypress guide accepts a free account or purchased-plan route.
The signup page exposes Continue with Google and a separate form route.
primary_path.output: Google sign-in flow or account form.
primary_path.success_signal: The chosen identity flow is open.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Identity-provider or form-access errors stop signup.

primary_path.action: For the form route, enter full name, business email, and password; otherwise complete Google sign-in.
details: The direct account form requires full name, business email, and password.
Google is a branch that supplies identity through the external provider.
primary_path.input: Required form identity fields or an eligible Google identity.
primary_path.success_signal: The page accepts the required identity values.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Invalid business email, password, or Google authentication prevents continuation.

primary_path.action: Accept the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy and submit account creation.
details: The page links the Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
Role and discovery questions can appear as additional profile prompts in the current signup experience.
primary_path.input: Completed identity flow and acceptance of linked policies.
primary_path.success_signal: BrowserStack accepts the signup submission.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Unaccepted policies, invalid inputs, or any live verification can delay account creation.

primary_path.action: Provision the BrowserStack account and expose the authenticated dashboard.
details: The signup page advertises 100 automated browser-testing minutes.
The current free-trial FAQ instead says Automate includes 60 minutes, so the exact starting allowance is not consistent across official pages.
primary_path.output: Authenticated BrowserStack account with available product entitlements.
primary_path.success_signal: The BrowserStack dashboard and account settings are accessible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Verification, organization, plan, or entitlement gates can block Automate.

primary_path.action: Open account settings and obtain the BrowserStack Username and Access key.
details: Both values authenticate the Cypress CLI run.
Treat the access key as a secret.
primary_path.output: Username and Access key available for configuration.
primary_path.success_signal: Both credential values can be copied.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing Automate access or revoked credentials prevent authentication.

primary_path.action: Install or verify Node.js 12 or newer, npm, and Git.
details: Node.js 12+ is an explicit prerequisite.
Git is required by the documented clone command even though it is not separately listed.
primary_path.output: Working node, npm, and git commands.
primary_path.success_signal: Version checks meet the guide's minimums and commands are on PATH.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Package installation or PATH issues stop setup.

primary_path.action: Install the BrowserStack Cypress CLI globally.
details: Run npm install -g browserstack-cypress-cli.
primary_path.input: Node.js, npm, network, and global install permission.
primary_path.success_signal: The command is available in the shell.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: npm registry, global prefix, permission, or PATH errors can block installation.

primary_path.action: Clone the Cypress Kitchen Sink repository and enter it.
details: For Cypress 10 and newer, run git clone https://github.com/cypress-io/cypress-example-kitchensink.git && cd cypress-example-kitchensink.
The Cypress 9-and-older branch uses an additional pinned commit checkout.
primary_path.success_signal: The repository structure includes app, cypress, and cypress.config.js.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Clone, network, existing-directory, or Git errors can fail setup.

primary_path.action: Install the Kitchen Sink project dependencies.
details: Run npm install.
primary_path.output: Installed node_modules and resolved project dependencies.
primary_path.success_signal: npm install completes.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Dependency, engine, lockfile, registry, native module, or network failures can block progress.

primary_path.action: Initialize BrowserStack Cypress configuration.
details: Run browserstack-cypress init.
This creates a boilerplate browserstack.json in the project.
primary_path.input: Installed BrowserStack Cypress CLI in the project directory.
primary_path.success_signal: The configuration file exists.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Wrong directory, CLI, or filesystem permission can fail initialization.

primary_path.action: Set BrowserStack credentials in browserstack.json.
details: Under auth, set username to the account Username and access_key to the Access key.
Do not commit real credentials to source control.
primary_path.success_signal: auth contains usable values.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Placeholder, revoked, exposed, or wrong-account credentials fail the run.

primary_path.action: Configure Cypress file, version, project, and build settings.
details: For current Cypress, set cypress_config_file to ./cypress.config.js; Cypress 9 and lower uses ./cypress.json.
The guide's sample uses cypress_version 13.latest, project_name Cypress Kitchen Sink Example, build_name Build no: 1, and parallels 5.
Add external npm packages under npm_dependencies only if required.
primary_path.output: Run settings for the sample build.
primary_path.success_signal: The config points to the actual Cypress config and uses a supported Cypress version.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Version or file mismatch prevents the runner from loading tests.

primary_path.action: Configure browser and operating-system combinations.
details: The sample includes Chrome on Windows 10, Firefox on OS X Mojave, and Edge on OS X Catalina with current or prior browser versions.
Use BrowserStack's supported devices and browsers rather than assuming every example combination remains available.
primary_path.success_signal: At least one supported browser combination is configured.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Unsupported OS/browser/version combinations fail scheduling.

primary_path.action: Choose public URL instead of localhost for the representative route.
details: The guide supports the public Cypress Kitchen Sink app or a locally hosted app.
The public URL is https://example.cypress.io/.
primary_path.input: Preference to avoid a local tunnel and app server.
primary_path.success_signal: The public setup instructions are selected.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Local testing has additional server and connection settings.

primary_path.action: Open the Kitchen Sink project in an IDE.
details: Use a project-wide search and replacement in the next action.
primary_path.success_signal: The project root is open.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Opening a subdirectory can miss spec files.

primary_path.action: Replace every localhost test URL with the public Cypress URL.
details: Search for http://localhost:8080.
Replace every instance with https://example.cypress.io.
Ensure all *.spec.js files are updated.
primary_path.success_signal: No required spec still points to localhost:8080.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A missed occurrence makes that test target an unavailable local server.

primary_path.action: Disable BrowserStack Local for the public route.
details: Set connection_settings.local to false in browserstack.json.
primary_path.success_signal: The configuration explicitly has local false.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Leaving local enabled can add an unnecessary tunnel dependency.

primary_path.action: Run the BrowserStack Cypress build.
details: Run browserstack-cypress run.
The CLI uploads configuration and tests, schedules the browser matrix, and streams completed test status.
primary_path.input: Configured project, credentials, supported matrix, and network.
primary_path.success_signal: The CLI starts and reports test execution across the configured browsers.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Credential, quota, parallel limit, configuration, upload, browser availability, or test failures can affect the build.

primary_path.action: Verify command-line completion and local result artifacts.
details: The CLI displays completed tests and their current status while the build runs.
After execution, results contains HTML and JSON files with the build summary.
primary_path.success_signal: Completed tests are reported and summary files exist.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A failed or interrupted run may produce incomplete results.

primary_path.action: Open the BrowserStack Automate dashboard.
details: Use the same account as the configured credentials.
primary_path.success_signal: The Cypress build appears.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Wrong organization, project, or credentials can hide it.

primary_path.action: Inspect the build grouped by browser combination.
details: Each spec's tests are grouped by browser combination.
Status is visible for each group.
primary_path.success_signal: Configured browser groups and test statuses are shown.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing groups can indicate unsupported configuration or scheduling failure.

primary_path.action: Open one browser combination and inspect its detailed logs.
details: The detailed view includes video and text logs.
Failed tests additionally show relevant screenshots in the Screenshots tab.
primary_path.success_signal: Video and text logs for the executed test are accessible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing evidence or no completed session means the first-test result is incomplete.

branches.condition: The developer chooses Continue with Google rather than the direct form.
branches.path: Authenticate with an eligible Google identity, then complete any BrowserStack profile prompts and accept the linked Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces manual full-name, business-email, and password entry with an external identity-provider handoff.

branches.condition: The sample must use Cypress 9 or older.
branches.path: Clone the same repository, enter it, check out commit ab10094ef7b199ae7febafec413a0626414bcd3c, install packages, set cypress_config_file to ./cypress.json, and use the corresponding integration-directory layout.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Pins older sample code and changes configuration and test-directory structure.

branches.condition: The developer tests the locally hosted Kitchen Sink app.
branches.path: Keep the http://localhost:8080 test URLs, set connection_settings.local true and local_identifier My_Sample_Tutorial, run npm start, verify the app at http://localhost:8080, then run browserstack-cypress run with Local connectivity.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a local app server, verification, BrowserStack Local tunnel behavior, and related network/firewall gates.

branches.condition: The developer selects Selenium, Playwright, Puppeteer, JS Testing API, Appium, Espresso, XCUITest, Flutter, Detox, Maestro, or another framework.
branches.path: Use the framework and language selector, then follow its official sample-build guide through account credentials, SDK or dependencies, sample repository, capabilities, run command, and dashboard verification.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes the code, runtime, framework, device/browser matrix, and result grouping.

branches.condition: The intended product is Live, App Live, Low Code Automation, accessibility, Percy, load testing, test management, or another BrowserStack product.
branches.path: Use that product's official get-started flow and terminal session, build, scan, report, or test result instead of Automate Cypress.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Replaces the entire setup and observable result.

friction_gates.description: BrowserStack has many products with unrelated first results.
Automate requires a framework, language, and sample-versus-existing-suite choice.
The guide requires a Free Account or purchased plan with Automate access, and signup requires Google identity or full name, business email, and password.
Account creation requires acceptance of BrowserStack's Terms of Service and Privacy Policy.
Two current official pages disagree on the free automated-browser allowance: the signup page says 100 minutes, while the FAQ says 60 Automate minutes.
The run requires BrowserStack Username and Access key.
The selected route requires Node.js 12+, npm, Git, the global BrowserStack Cypress CLI, and project dependencies.
The sample config stores account credentials in browserstack.json, creating a secret-handling risk.
The developer must choose supported browser, version, operating-system, and parallel combinations.
Public versus localhost changes URL editing, tunnel, and app-server work.
The remote browser matrix must schedule and complete before detailed results exist.

sources.title: Run your first Cypress test
evidence_supported: Complete representative account-to-result path, branches, credentials, commands, config, and terminal evidence

sources.title: Get started with Selenium
sections_used: Test your locally hosted web apps
evidence_supported: Sample, existing suite, local testing, framework, and language choices

sources.title: Create and run your first test
evidence_supported: Concrete low-code alternative and its different desktop, recording, local replay, suite, and cloud result path

sources.title: Create FREE Account
evidence_supported: Google or direct account route, full name, business email, password, Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, profile prompts, and advertised 100 automated browser-testing minutes

sources.title: What do I get with a free trial?
evidence_supported: 30 minutes Live, 30 minutes App Live, 60 minutes Automate, 100 minutes App Automate, and 5,000 Percy screenshots

uncertainties.question: Which BrowserStack product and test result should represent the platform?
uncertainties.impact: A manual session, automated browser build, mobile app session, accessibility scan, visual diff, low-code recording, load test, or report has different prerequisites and success.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The company and docs expose many products, and the roster supplies no test intent.

uncertainties.question: Which Automate framework, language, browser matrix, and application target applies?
uncertainties.impact: These choices change dependencies, credentials placement, sample code, commands, scheduling, and result evidence.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official overview requires framework and language selection; Cypress and public Kitchen Sink are representative only.

uncertainties.question: Which official free automated-browser allowance applies to a newly created account?
uncertainties.impact: A 60-minute rather than 100-minute allowance changes how much of a sample matrix can complete before quota exhaustion.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current signup page advertises 100 automated browser-testing minutes, while the current free-trial FAQ lists 60 Automate minutes.

uncertainties.question: Does the live signup flow add an email verification, organization-creation, or entitlement screen after submission?
uncertainties.impact: Any post-submit gate would add another access or wait step before BrowserStack credentials are available.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The public signup page exposes its current identity fields and policy acceptance, but the authenticated post-submit state cannot be observed without creating an account.

uncertainties.question: Are all operating-system examples in the current sample browserstack.json still schedulable for the account?
uncertainties.impact: An obsolete or plan-restricted matrix can fail before a first result.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide tells the developer to consult supported browsers and devices rather than guaranteeing every sample combination for every account.

excluded_after_success: Integrating the developer's existing test suite.
Running tests in parallel beyond the sample configuration and optimizing concurrency.
Testing a private or local application when the public branch is selected.
Advanced capabilities, geolocation, network conditions, browsers and devices, CI integration, debugging, analytics, AI agents, and production secret management.
